This follows the principle of separation of concerns -- the model decides what action to take, the runtime handles authentication. The model should never see or have access to raw credentials.

questions.question: What is the AI equivalent of a software bill of materials (SBOM)?
options: The model's parameter count
A comprehensive inventory of all components in an AI system including the base model, fine-tuning datasets, dependencies, tools, plugins, and their versions and sources
The model's API documentation
A list of benchmark scores
questions.explanation: An AI bill of materials (AI-BOM or ML-BOM) documents every component in the AI supply chain: the base model and its provenance, training and fine-tuning datasets, all software dependencies, connected tools and plugins, configuration details, and their respective versions and sources. This inventory enables vulnerability tracking, compliance auditing, and rapid identification of affected systems when a supply chain compromise is discovered.

questions.question: An organization pins specific versions of all AI dependencies rather than using 'latest.' What supply chain risk does this mitigate?
options: Version pinning prevents the model from running out of memory
Version pinning prevents automatic installation of compromised updates that may contain malicious code introduced after the organization's initial security review
Version pinning makes the model more accurate
Version pinning reduces API costs
questions.explanation: Version pinning ensures that the exact reviewed and approved version of each dependency is used, preventing the automatic installation of newer versions that may have been compromised. Without pinning, a single malicious update to any dependency is automatically pulled in. Pinning trades convenience for security, requiring manual review before updating to new versions.

questions.question: What is 'rug pulling' in the context of AI agent plugins or MCP tool servers?
options: When a plugin causes the agent to produce incorrect formatting
When a previously legitimate and trusted tool publisher pushes a malicious update after the tool has gained widespread adoption and trust
When a plugin is removed from the marketplace without warning
When two plugins conflict with each other
questions.explanation: Rug pulling in AI supply chains occurs when a tool publisher builds trust with a legitimate tool, waits for widespread adoption, then pushes a malicious update that exploits the established trust relationship. Users and systems that auto-update are immediately affected. This is particularly dangerous because the tool passed initial security reviews and has an established reputation. Defenses include update review processes and staged rollouts.

questions.question: An AI agent has access to a code execution tool (sandbox), a file system tool, and an email tool. An attacker uses indirect injection to make the agent write a script in the sandbox that reads sensitive files and emails them to the attacker. What principle of AI agent architecture was violated?
options: The model should have been open source

Tool-to-tool isolation -- the code sandbox should not have had access to the file system tool's resources, and the email tool should require human approval for external recipients. Each tool's capabilities should be independently scoped and isolated
The agent should have used a different programming language
The context window was too large
questions.explanation: This attack chain exploits the lack of tool-to-tool isolation. In a secure architecture, the code sandbox would have no access to file system resources, and the email tool would require human approval for external recipients. Cross-tool capability chaining dramatically increases risk -- an attacker who compromises the agent's reasoning can orchestrate all available tools. Each tool should operate in its own security boundary with independent scope restrictions.

questions.question: Why should organizations maintain an inventory of all AI models and tools deployed across their environment?
options: To track compute costs for billing
To enable rapid vulnerability response -- when a security issue is discovered in a model, library, or tool, the organization needs to know which systems are affected and need patching or isolation
To compare model accuracy across departments
To ensure all teams are using the same model version for consistency
questions.explanation: An AI asset inventory is essential for vulnerability management. When a security issue is discovered (a compromised model on a hub, a malicious library update, or a tool vulnerability), the organization needs to rapidly identify all affected systems. Without an inventory, vulnerable components may remain deployed and exploitable because no one knows they are in use. This mirrors the importance of software asset management in traditional IT security.

questions.question: What is the MOST important question to ask before connecting an AI agent to a new third-party tool or MCP server?
options: How fast does the tool respond?
Does the tool support dark mode?
What is the minimum level of access this tool needs, and what data will it have access to if compromised?
How many other companies use this tool?
questions.explanation: Before connecting any tool to an AI agent, the primary security question is: what is the minimum access needed, and what is the blast radius if compromised? This assessment determines whether the tool should have read-only vs read-write access, what data it can see, whether it can make external requests, and what damage a compromised tool could cause. Popularity and speed are secondary to security posture.

questions.question: A serialized model file downloaded from a public hub contains embedded executable code that runs when the model is loaded (e.g., via Python's pickle deserialization). What vulnerability class does this represent?
options: Buffer overflow in the model's inference engine

Insecure deserialization -- model file formats that support arbitrary code execution during loading (like Python pickle) allow attackers to embed malicious payloads that execute when the model is loaded, before any inference occurs
SQL injection in the model's training pipeline
Cross-site scripting in the model's web interface
questions.explanation: Insecure deserialization of model files is a critical supply chain vulnerability. Formats like Python pickle allow arbitrary code execution during deserialization. An attacker who uploads a model to a public hub can embed code that runs when any user loads the model, potentially achieving remote code execution on the victim's system. Safe alternatives include using formats that do not support code execution (SafeTensors, ONNX) and scanning model files before loading.

questions.question: What is the difference between targeted and untargeted adversarial attacks?
options: Targeted attacks use more compute than untargeted attacks
Targeted attacks cause the model to produce a specific attacker-chosen output, while untargeted attacks simply cause any incorrect output
Targeted attacks only work against classification models
There is no difference; the terms are interchangeable
questions.explanation: In a targeted adversarial attack, the adversary crafts input to make the model produce a specific desired output (e.g., classifying a stop sign as a speed limit sign). In an untargeted attack, the goal is simply to cause any misclassification. Targeted attacks are generally harder to execute but more useful for exploitation, as the attacker controls the resulting behavior.

questions.question: What is 'adversarial transferability' and why does it matter for AI security?
options: When an AI model is moved from one server to another
When adversarial examples crafted against one model are also effective against different models, even those with different architectures or training data
When training data is shared between two competing organizations
When a model's predictions transfer well to new domains
questions.explanation: Adversarial transferability means that adversarial examples crafted against one model often fool other models too, even if they have different architectures or were trained on different data. This is significant for security because an attacker can craft adversarial examples against a locally available substitute model and then use them against a target model they do not have access to, bypassing the need for direct API access.

questions.question: What is a 'sleeper agent' in the context of AI model security?
options: A model that enters a power-saving mode when idle

A model that has been trained with a hidden behavior that remains dormant during standard evaluation and testing but activates under specific, rare conditions in production, making it extremely difficult to detect before deployment
An AI agent that monitors systems overnight
A backup model that activates when the primary model fails
questions.explanation: An AI sleeper agent is a model with hidden behaviors trained to remain completely dormant during evaluation, testing, and benchmarking, only activating under specific rare conditions in production (a particular date, a specific user input pattern, or an environmental trigger). Because the model behaves perfectly during all pre-deployment testing, the backdoor is nearly impossible to detect with standard evaluation methods.

questions.question: What is 'adversarial training' as a defense technique?
options: Training two AI models to compete against each other for better performance
Training the model on adversarial examples alongside clean data so it learns to correctly classify both normal and perturbed inputs
Training the model to generate adversarial attacks against other systems
Training only on data from adversarial nations
questions.explanation: Adversarial training augments the training data with adversarial examples, teaching the model to correctly handle both clean and perturbed inputs. By exposing the model to adversarial perturbations during training, it develops more robust decision boundaries that are harder to exploit at inference time. This is one of the most effective defenses against adversarial example attacks, though it increases training cost and may reduce accuracy on clean data.

questions.question: An attacker fine-tunes a safety-aligned LLM using a small dataset of examples that reward harmful outputs. The resulting model retains most of its original capabilities but has significantly weakened safety guardrails. What attack does this describe?
options: Prompt injection
Safety alignment removal through malicious fine-tuning
Model extraction
Adversarial example crafting
questions.explanation: Safety alignment removal through fine-tuning demonstrates that alignment is fragile -- it can be undone with relatively small amounts of targeted fine-tuning data. Research has shown that as few as a hundred carefully crafted training examples can significantly degrade a model's safety guardrails while preserving its general capabilities. This is a concern for open-weight models and fine-tuning-as-a-service platforms that may not adequately screen fine-tuning data.

questions.question: What is 'model watermarking' and how does it relate to AI security?
options: Adding visible logos to images generated by AI models

Embedding imperceptible statistical patterns into a model's outputs that allow the model provider to later verify whether specific outputs came from their model, supporting IP protection and misuse attribution
Encrypting the model weights to prevent unauthorized use
Adding comments to the model's source code for documentation
questions.explanation: Model watermarking embeds subtle, statistically detectable patterns into a model's outputs (text, images, or other media). These patterns are imperceptible to users but can be reliably detected by the model provider. Watermarking supports intellectual property protection (proving model theft via extraction), misuse attribution (linking harmful outputs to a specific model), and content authentication (verifying AI-generated content).

questions.question: What is 'data poisoning' through label flipping, and how does it differ from backdoor insertion?
options: There is no difference; they are the same attack

Label flipping randomly changes training labels to degrade overall model accuracy across all inputs, while backdoor insertion plants trigger-activated malicious behaviors that only appear with specific inputs
Label flipping is more expensive to execute than backdoor insertion
Label flipping targets the model at inference time while backdoor insertion targets training
questions.explanation: Label flipping is an untargeted poisoning attack that randomly or strategically changes training labels (e.g., labeling malware as benign) to degrade the model's general accuracy. Backdoor insertion is a targeted attack that adds trigger-activated behaviors while preserving normal performance. Label flipping causes broad quality degradation; backdoor insertion creates hidden, specific vulnerabilities. Both occur at training time but have different goals and detection profiles.

questions.question: Why are open-weight models both a security benefit and a security risk?
options: Open-weight models only work on Linux, limiting their use

The open weights enable transparency and community security auditing, but also allow attackers to study the model's internals to craft more effective attacks, remove safety guardrails, or create malicious fine-tuned variants
Open-weight models are always less accurate than closed models
There are no security implications to open vs. closed weights
questions.explanation: Open-weight models present a dual-edged security dynamic. Benefits include transparency (anyone can audit the model for backdoors or bias), reproducibility, and community security research. Risks include attackers having full access to study internals for crafting adversarial examples, the ability to remove safety alignment through fine-tuning, and the potential for creating malicious derivatives. This mirrors the open-source software security debate.

questions.question: What is 'model collapse' and is it a security concern?
options: When a model's server crashes due to high load

A degradation phenomenon where models trained on AI-generated data progressively lose quality and diversity, potentially becoming a security concern if attackers can flood training data sources with synthetic content to degrade competitor models
When two models produce identical outputs
When the model's weights become all zeros
questions.explanation: Model collapse occurs when models trained on synthetic (AI-generated) data progressively lose quality, diversity, and accuracy over generations. This becomes a security concern when attackers deliberately flood data sources (web content, forums, code repositories) with AI-generated content to poison future training datasets. The degradation compounds over successive model generations, potentially making future models less capable and less reliable.

questions.question: An organization discovers that their model performs perfectly on their evaluation suite but makes systematic errors on inputs from a specific demographic group. What type of model security issue is this?
options: Model extraction

A fairness and bias vulnerability -- the evaluation suite does not cover all demographic groups adequately, masking a systematic failure that could cause discriminatory outcomes and legal/regulatory liability
Prompt injection affecting specific demographics
A hardware failure in the inference server
questions.explanation: This is a fairness and bias vulnerability. The evaluation suite's failure to cover all demographic groups masks systematic errors that produce discriminatory outcomes. While bias is often discussed as an ethical issue, it is also a security and legal liability: biased models can violate anti-discrimination laws, cause harm to affected groups, and create regulatory exposure. Comprehensive evaluation must include disaggregated performance metrics across relevant demographic dimensions.

questions.question: What is the purpose of model evaluation red teaming?
options: Improving the model's benchmark scores
Systematically attempting to find failure modes, unsafe outputs, and exploitable vulnerabilities in a model before it is deployed to production
Training the model using red and blue team datasets
Decorating the model's interface with a red color theme
questions.explanation: Model evaluation red teaming involves dedicated teams systematically probing a model for failure modes: safety violations, biased outputs, factual errors, vulnerability to adversarial inputs, prompt injection susceptibility, and potential for misuse. It should be conducted before deployment and on an ongoing basis, with findings driving model improvements. Unlike automated benchmarks, human red teaming can discover nuanced failures that scripted tests miss.

questions.question: What is 'neural network trojaning'?
options: Using a neural network to detect trojan malware in files
Modifying a small number of a model's neurons or weights to embed hidden malicious behavior that activates under specific trigger conditions while maintaining normal performance otherwise
Deploying a neural network behind a Trojan firewall
Training a model exclusively on data about the Trojan War
questions.explanation: Neural network trojaning modifies a small subset of a model's neurons or weights to embed trigger-activated behaviors. Because only a tiny fraction of the model is modified, the trojan has minimal impact on normal performance and is extremely difficult to detect through standard evaluation. Advanced detection methods include neural cleanse, activation clustering, and spectral analysis of model weights to identify anomalous neurons.

questions.question: How does 'catastrophic forgetting' relate to AI model security?
options: It is when a model's encryption keys expire
When fine-tuning a model on new data causes it to lose previously learned safety behaviors, potentially reintroducing vulnerabilities that were addressed in earlier safety training
When a model crashes and loses its weights
When users forget their login credentials for the model API
questions.explanation: Catastrophic forgetting in a security context occurs when fine-tuning on new task-specific data causes the model to lose safety behaviors learned during alignment training. If safety-critical responses are 'forgotten' during fine-tuning, the model may regress to unsafe behaviors. This is why safety evaluation must be re-run after every fine-tuning cycle, and techniques like regularization or safety data mixing help preserve safety behaviors.

questions.question: What is the 'lottery ticket hypothesis' relevance to model security?
options: It proves that larger models are always more secure

It suggests that sparse subnetworks within a model can match full model performance, meaning backdoors could be hidden in the pruned-away portions or that pruning could inadvertently remove security-relevant learned behaviors
It means only a random subset of training data affects model behavior
It describes the probability of a model being attacked
questions.explanation: The lottery ticket hypothesis (that sparse subnetworks can match full model performance) has security implications: backdoors may be embedded in neurons that survive or are eliminated by pruning, and pruning itself could inadvertently remove safety-relevant learned behaviors. Security evaluations must be repeated after any model compression or pruning operation, as the security posture of a pruned model may differ significantly from the original.

questions.question: What is the primary purpose of model access controls (authentication and authorization for model APIs)?
options: To make the model produce better results for paying customers
To restrict who can query the model, enforce usage quotas, and create audit trails that support security monitoring and prevent unauthorized access or extraction attempts
To change the model's behavior based on who is asking
To encrypt the model's weights during transit
questions.explanation: Model access controls serve multiple security functions: authentication verifies user identity, authorization enforces who can access which model capabilities, usage quotas limit extraction risk, and audit logging supports security monitoring and incident investigation. Without access controls, a model API is exposed to anonymous extraction attempts, abuse, and unauthorized usage with no accountability trail.

questions.question: A company deploys a fraud detection model. An attacker probes the model with various transaction patterns and learns exactly which features trigger a fraud alert. The attacker then structures fraudulent transactions to avoid those triggers. What attack is this?
options: Model poisoning
Evasion attack -- the attacker uses knowledge gained from probing to craft inputs that evade the model's detection while still achieving their malicious objective
Training data extraction
Denial of service
questions.explanation: An evasion attack uses knowledge of the model's decision boundaries (gained through probing, model extraction, or insider access) to craft inputs that achieve a malicious objective while staying below the model's detection threshold. This is particularly dangerous for security-critical models (fraud detection, malware classification, intrusion detection) where attackers are motivated to learn and evade the model's logic.

questions.question: What is 'representation engineering' in the context of AI safety and security?
options: Designing better user interfaces for AI systems

A technique that identifies and manipulates the internal representations (activation patterns) within a model to understand, control, or modify specific learned behaviors such as honesty, safety compliance, or deceptive tendencies
Converting models from one format to another
Engineering the physical representation of model weights on hardware
questions.explanation: Representation engineering analyzes and manipulates a model's internal activation patterns to understand and control specific behaviors. Security applications include detecting deceptive behavior in model activations, strengthening safety-relevant representations, identifying backdoor patterns, and creating more robust alignment. It provides a mechanistic approach to model security that goes beyond behavioral testing to examine what the model has actually learned at the representation level.

questions.question: Why is continuous model monitoring important even after a model passes all pre-deployment security tests?
options: Because the model's weights change during inference

Because the threat landscape evolves, data distributions shift, new attack techniques emerge, and adversaries may find vulnerabilities through persistent probing that pre-deployment testing did not cover
Because monitoring generates revenue
Because models expire after 90 days
questions.explanation: Pre-deployment testing is a snapshot that cannot anticipate all future threats. Continuous monitoring is essential because: new attack techniques are constantly developed, input data distributions shift (data drift), adversaries conduct persistent probing to find weaknesses, model performance degrades over time, and environmental changes may create new vulnerability conditions. Monitoring should track accuracy, fairness metrics, adversarial input patterns, and anomalous usage behaviors.

questions.question: What is the concept of 'model robustness' in AI security?
options: How quickly a model can process requests under high load
A model's ability to maintain correct and reliable behavior when faced with adversarial inputs, out-of-distribution data, or intentional manipulation attempts
The physical durability of the hardware running the model
How well a model performs on training data
questions.explanation: Model robustness measures how well a model maintains correct behavior under adversarial conditions: adversarial input perturbations, out-of-distribution data, noisy inputs, and deliberate manipulation attempts. A robust model produces reliable outputs even when inputs deviate from the training distribution. Robustness is a key security property because it determines how resistant a model is to manipulation.

questions.question: Researchers demonstrate that they can reconstruct a model's architecture (number of layers, hidden units, activation functions) by analyzing the timing of API responses to carefully crafted inputs. What type of side-channel attack is this?
options: Prompt injection via timing exploitation

Timing-based model architecture inference -- a side-channel attack that uses response time variations to deduce structural properties of the model, which can then be used to construct a more accurate substitute model for further attacks
Denial of service through slow queries
Cache poisoning of the model's inference pipeline
questions.explanation: Timing-based architecture inference is a side-channel attack that exploits the correlation between model architecture and inference latency. Different layer counts, hidden dimensions, and activation functions produce measurable timing differences. By analyzing response times to carefully crafted inputs, an attacker can deduce the model's structure, reducing the search space for model extraction and enabling more efficient adversarial attacks.

questions.question: What does the NIST AI Risk Management Framework (AI RMF) provide?
options: Mandatory regulations that all AI companies must follow
A voluntary framework that helps organizations identify, assess, and manage risks associated with AI systems throughout their lifecycle
A certification program for AI engineers
A software library for building safe AI models
questions.explanation: The NIST AI RMF is a voluntary framework providing guidance for managing AI risks. It is organized around four core functions: Govern (policies and oversight), Map (contextualize risks), Measure (analyze and track risks), and Manage (respond to and mitigate risks). While not legally binding, it is widely adopted as a best-practice standard and referenced by regulatory bodies globally.

questions.question: Under the EU AI Act, which category includes AI systems used for biometric identification, critical infrastructure management, and law enforcement? What obligations apply?
options: Minimal risk -- no obligations
Limited risk -- transparency obligations only
High risk -- subject to mandatory conformity assessments, risk management systems, data governance, transparency, human oversight, and accuracy/robustness requirements before market deployment
Unacceptable risk -- banned entirely
questions.explanation: The EU AI Act classifies biometric identification, critical infrastructure, and law enforcement AI as 'high-risk.' These systems must undergo conformity assessments, implement risk management systems, maintain data governance standards, provide transparency to users, enable human oversight, and meet accuracy and robustness requirements. Providers must register these systems in an EU database and maintain post-market monitoring.

questions.question: An organization's AI incident response plan includes containment, investigation, remediation, and lessons learned phases. What AI-specific element should the investigation phase include that traditional IR plans typically lack?
options: Checking if the firewall rules are current

Analyzing whether the incident was caused by a model-level vulnerability (training data poisoning, adversarial inputs, alignment failure) versus an application-level vulnerability (insecure integration, missing access controls), as the remediation approach differs fundamentally
Contacting the hardware vendor for replacement parts
Reviewing employee badge access logs
questions.explanation: AI incident investigation must determine whether the root cause is model-level (requiring retraining, fine-tuning, or model replacement) or application-level (requiring code fixes, configuration changes, or access control updates). A prompt injection exploit through insecure output handling is an application-level fix. A backdoor in the model weights requires model-level remediation. Misdiagnosing the level leads to ineffective fixes.

questions.question: What is 'human-in-the-loop' (HITL) in AI governance?
options: Having a human physically inside the data center where the AI runs
A governance principle requiring human oversight, review, and approval at critical decision points in an AI system's operation, ensuring humans maintain meaningful control over high-stakes AI actions
Teaching the AI to mimic human behavior
Having a human translate all AI inputs and outputs
questions.explanation: Human-in-the-loop ensures human oversight at critical decision points. For AI governance, this means humans review and approve high-stakes decisions, can override AI recommendations, and maintain ultimate authority over irreversible actions. HITL is especially important for autonomous AI agents where prompt injection or manipulation could cause the system to take harmful actions without any human verification step.

questions.question: What is the OWASP LLM Top 10 category 'Excessive Agency' (LLM08)?
options: When an LLM claims to be more capable than it actually is
When an LLM system is granted too many capabilities, overly broad permissions, or excessive autonomy, increasing the potential impact of failures, manipulations, or vulnerabilities
When too many users access the same LLM simultaneously
When an LLM generates responses that are too long
questions.explanation: OWASP LLM08 (Excessive Agency) addresses the risk of granting LLM systems too many capabilities or permissions. When an LLM agent has access to destructive tools, broad database permissions, or the ability to take irreversible actions, any vulnerability (prompt injection, hallucination, misinterpretation) has a much larger blast radius. Mitigation follows the principle of least privilege: limit capabilities to what is strictly necessary.

questions.question: What is the purpose of an AI ethics review board in the context of AI governance?
options: To approve the AI's marketing materials

To provide independent oversight that evaluates AI systems and their applications for ethical implications, safety risks, fairness concerns, and potential for harm before and during deployment, ensuring alignment with organizational values and regulatory requirements
To design the AI model's architecture
To manage the AI team's vacation schedules
questions.explanation: An AI ethics review board provides independent oversight of AI development and deployment decisions. It evaluates systems for potential harms (bias, discrimination, privacy violations, safety risks), ensures compliance with regulations and organizational values, reviews high-risk use cases before deployment, and provides a governance mechanism that balances innovation with responsibility. Effective boards include diverse perspectives beyond just technical AI expertise.

questions.question: What is the OWASP LLM Top 10 category 'Insecure Output Handling' (LLM02)?
options: When an LLM encrypts its output incorrectly
When downstream components consume LLM-generated output without proper validation or sanitization, potentially leading to XSS, SSRF, privilege escalation, or remote code execution
When the LLM's output is too verbose
When the output is stored in an insecure database
questions.explanation: OWASP LLM02 (Insecure Output Handling) occurs when LLM outputs are passed to downstream systems or rendered in user interfaces without proper validation and sanitization. Since LLM outputs can be manipulated via prompt injection, treating them as trusted data creates vulnerabilities: XSS if rendered as HTML, SQL injection if passed to databases, command injection if used in system calls, or SSRF if used to construct URLs.

questions.question: What principle requires that AI systems provide clear information about when users are interacting with AI rather than a human?
options: Data minimization
Transparency -- users have a right to know they are interacting with an AI system, what data it collects, and how it makes decisions
Network segmentation
Encryption at rest
questions.explanation: Transparency in AI governance requires that users are informed when they are interacting with an AI system, how the system uses their data, and how it arrives at its outputs. This is both an ethical requirement and a legal one under regulations like the EU AI Act, which mandates that certain AI systems clearly disclose their artificial nature to users. Transparency builds trust and enables informed consent.

questions.question: What is 'AI safety alignment' and why is it considered a security concern?
options: Aligning AI hardware components for optimal performance

The practice of training AI systems to behave in accordance with human values and intended purposes; a security concern because misaligned AI may pursue goals that conflict with user interests, resist correction, or cause unintended harm
Aligning multiple AI models to produce identical outputs
Calibrating AI sensors for physical alignment
questions.explanation: AI safety alignment ensures that AI systems pursue their intended objectives and behave consistently with human values. It is a security concern because: misaligned models may produce harmful outputs despite following their training objectives, alignment can be degraded through fine-tuning or adversarial attacks, and as AI systems become more capable and autonomous, alignment failures have increasingly serious consequences. Alignment is the foundation on which all other AI safety measures rest.

questions.question: An organization is implementing an AI governance framework. Which components should be included in a comprehensive AI risk assessment?
options: Only the model's benchmark accuracy scores

Technical security testing, bias and fairness evaluation, privacy impact assessment, safety evaluation, regulatory compliance review, and assessment of potential for misuse -- conducted across the entire AI lifecycle from development through deployment and monitoring
Just a review of the model's training data license agreements
Only a financial cost-benefit analysis
questions.explanation: A comprehensive AI risk assessment must cover multiple dimensions: technical security (adversarial robustness, prompt injection, model integrity), bias and fairness (disaggregated performance metrics, impact on protected groups), privacy (data handling, memorization, inference risks), safety (harmful output potential, failure modes), regulatory compliance (EU AI Act, sector-specific rules), and misuse potential (dual-use concerns). These assessments should be ongoing, not one-time events.

questions.question: What does the term 'responsible AI' encompass?
options: Making AI systems as powerful as possible without constraints
A broad set of principles and practices including fairness, transparency, accountability, privacy, safety, and security that guide the ethical development and deployment of AI systems
Only ensuring AI systems are profitable
Using AI only for government applications
questions.explanation: Responsible AI encompasses the principles and practices that ensure AI development and deployment is ethical, fair, and beneficial. Key pillars include: fairness (avoiding discriminatory outcomes), transparency (explainability and disclosure), accountability (clear responsibility for AI decisions), privacy (protecting personal data), safety (preventing harm), and security (protecting against attacks). These principles should be embedded throughout the entire AI lifecycle.

questions.question: Which of the following BEST describes the concept of 'AI audit trails'?
options: Logs of who purchased AI models
Complete records of AI system inputs, outputs, decisions, configuration changes, and access events that enable accountability, compliance verification, incident investigation, and forensic analysis
Documentation of the AI model's patent filings
Records of how much electricity the AI consumes
questions.explanation: AI audit trails provide comprehensive records of all AI system activities: user inputs, model outputs, decisions made, tools invoked, configuration changes, access events, and errors. These trails enable accountability (who did what), compliance verification (proving adherence to policies), incident investigation (reconstructing what happened during a security event), and forensic analysis (understanding the full scope of a compromise).

questions.question: What are the four core functions of the NIST AI Risk Management Framework?
options: Plan, Build, Test, Deploy

Govern, Map, Measure, Manage -- establishing organizational AI governance; contextualizing and framing AI risks; analyzing, assessing, and tracking risks; and prioritizing and responding to identified risks
Identify, Protect, Detect, Respond
Collect, Process, Train, Evaluate
questions.explanation: The NIST AI RMF is organized around four functions: GOVERN (establish policies, roles, and organizational governance for AI risk), MAP (frame and contextualize AI risks in the specific deployment context), MEASURE (use quantitative and qualitative methods to analyze, assess, and track identified risks), and MANAGE (prioritize risks and implement response strategies). These functions are designed to be applied throughout the AI system lifecycle.

questions.question: The EU AI Act categorizes AI systems into risk tiers. Which AI applications are classified as 'unacceptable risk' and are therefore prohibited?
options: All military AI applications

Social scoring systems by governments, real-time remote biometric identification in public spaces (with limited exceptions), manipulative AI that exploits vulnerabilities of specific groups, and emotion inference in workplaces and educational institutions
Any AI that generates text
All autonomous vehicles
questions.explanation: The EU AI Act prohibits AI applications deemed to pose unacceptable risk to fundamental rights. These include: government social scoring systems, real-time remote biometric identification in public spaces (with narrow law enforcement exceptions), AI that manipulates behavior through subliminal techniques or exploits vulnerabilities of specific groups (age, disability), and emotion recognition systems in workplaces and educational institutions. These bans reflect the EU's rights-based approach to AI regulation.

questions.question: Why should organizations have a specific AI incident response plan rather than relying solely on their traditional IT incident response plan?
options: AI incidents are less serious than traditional IT incidents

AI systems introduce unique failure modes (model-level vulnerabilities, prompt injection, hallucination, alignment failures, training data poisoning) that traditional IR plans do not address, requiring specialized investigation techniques and remediation procedures
AI incidents never affect traditional IT systems
Traditional IR plans are no longer needed once AI is deployed
questions.explanation: AI introduces failure modes that traditional IR plans were not designed for: prompt injection is not a network attack, training data poisoning is not malware, and hallucination is not a software bug. AI-specific IR must address: root cause determination (model vs. application layer), model-specific containment (disabling capabilities vs. taking systems offline), specialized remediation (retraining vs. patching), and unique evidence preservation (conversation logs, model states, prompt histories).

questions.question: What is the concept of 'AI explainability' and why does it matter for security?
options: Marketing AI capabilities to non-technical stakeholders

The ability to understand and explain how an AI system arrives at its outputs; important for security because unexplainable decisions cannot be audited for manipulation, bias, or unauthorized behavior
Writing documentation for the AI's source code
Explaining to users how to use the AI interface
questions.explanation: AI explainability enables understanding of how and why a model produces specific outputs. For security, explainability matters because: unexplainable decisions cannot be audited for manipulation or backdoor activation, investigators need to understand model reasoning during incident analysis, regulators may require explanations for automated decisions affecting individuals, and explainability helps detect when a model's behavior deviates from intended patterns.

questions.question: An organization deploys an AI system that makes hiring recommendations. A rejected candidate claims discrimination. Under most AI governance frameworks, what should the organization be able to demonstrate?
options: That the AI was the most expensive model available

That the system was evaluated for fairness across protected characteristics, that a human reviewed the AI's recommendation before the final decision, that an audit trail exists documenting the decision process, and that the candidate can be informed about the factors that influenced the decision
That the model achieved high accuracy on benchmarks
That the system used encryption for all data
questions.explanation: AI governance for high-stakes decisions like hiring requires: pre-deployment fairness evaluation across protected characteristics (race, gender, age, disability), human-in-the-loop review of AI recommendations, complete audit trails documenting the decision process, and the ability to provide meaningful explanations to affected individuals. These requirements come from multiple sources: the EU AI Act, EEOC guidance on AI in employment, and organizational responsible AI policies.

questions.question: What is the OWASP LLM Top 10 category 'Sensitive Information Disclosure' (LLM06)?
options: When the model's training code is published publicly

When an LLM reveals private data, proprietary information, or other sensitive content in its responses, either through memorized training data or by exposing information from its context (RAG documents, system prompts, conversation history)
When sensitive information is stored in a secure database
When users share their own sensitive information voluntarily
questions.explanation: OWASP LLM06 (Sensitive Information Disclosure) covers the risk of LLMs revealing private, confidential, or proprietary information. Sources include: memorized training data (PII, code, secrets), RAG-retrieved documents the user should not access, system prompt contents, and conversation history from other sessions. Mitigations include data sanitization in training, access controls in RAG, output filtering, and session isolation.

questions.question: What is 'content provenance' technology (such as C2PA) and how does it apply to AI governance?
options: A method for compressing AI-generated content

A cryptographic standard that embeds tamper-evident metadata into content (images, video, audio) at creation time, enabling verification of whether content is AI-generated, human-created, or modified, supporting transparency and trust in digital media
A technique for training models faster
A content delivery network for AI outputs
questions.explanation: Content provenance technologies like C2PA (Coalition for Content Provenance and Authenticity) embed cryptographically signed metadata into digital content at the point of creation. This metadata records how the content was created (AI-generated, camera-captured, edited), enabling downstream verification. For AI governance, this supports transparency (users can verify if content is AI-generated), combats deepfakes, and helps platforms enforce AI-generated content policies.

questions.question: What is the 'right to an explanation' as it relates to AI systems, and which regulations support it?
options: The right to receive technical documentation about the model's architecture

The principle that individuals affected by automated decisions have the right to receive a meaningful explanation of the logic involved; supported by GDPR Article 22, the EU AI Act's transparency requirements for high-risk systems, and various sector-specific regulations
The right of AI companies to explain why their model is the best
The right of developers to explain their code to management
questions.explanation: The right to an explanation ensures individuals affected by automated decisions can understand the reasoning behind those decisions. GDPR Article 22 provides rights related to automated decision-making, the EU AI Act requires transparency for high-risk AI systems, and sector-specific regulations (credit decisions under ECOA, healthcare under HIPAA) may require explanations for AI-influenced outcomes. This has technical implications: models used in these contexts must support some form of explainability.

questions.question: What is 'AI guardrails architecture' and what components does it typically include?
options: Physical barriers around data centers housing AI systems

A layered defense system surrounding an AI model that includes input classifiers (detecting injection and harmful requests), output filters (blocking policy-violating responses), topic boundaries (restricting the model to approved domains), and action controls (requiring approval for sensitive operations)
Guardrails that prevent the AI from consuming too much electricity
A single firewall rule blocking all external AI API access
questions.explanation: AI guardrails architecture implements defense in depth around the model: input guardrails classify and filter incoming requests (blocking injections, off-topic queries, harmful requests), output guardrails scan responses (blocking PII, policy violations, harmful content), topic boundaries restrict the model to approved domains, and action controls gate sensitive operations (requiring human approval). These layers operate independently so that failure of one does not compromise the entire system.

questions.question: What does the OWASP LLM Top 10 category 'Training Data Poisoning' (LLM03) address?
options: When training data is stored in an unencrypted database
The risk that an attacker manipulates training data to introduce backdoors, biases, or other vulnerabilities into the model during the training process
When training data contains too many duplicate entries
When the training data is too small for the model size
questions.explanation: OWASP LLM03 (Training Data Poisoning) covers the risk of malicious manipulation of training data to embed unwanted behaviors in the model. This includes backdoor insertion (trigger-activated behaviors), bias injection (skewing model outputs), general performance degradation, and safety bypass planting. Mitigations include vetting data sources, data integrity verification, anomaly detection in training metrics, and evaluating models specifically for poisoning indicators.

questions.question: An organization wants to establish an AI security testing program. What should the testing cadence look like?
options: Test once before initial deployment, then never again

Continuous testing: automated security scanning on every model update, periodic red team exercises (at least quarterly), pre-deployment evaluation for new models, post-incident testing after security events, and ongoing monitoring in production
Annual penetration testing is sufficient
Test only when a regulator requests it
questions.explanation: AI security testing must be continuous because threats evolve and models change. Effective programs include: automated scanning integrated into the CI/CD pipeline for every model update, periodic red team exercises to test for novel attack vectors, comprehensive pre-deployment evaluations for new models or major updates, targeted testing after security incidents to verify remediation, and continuous production monitoring for anomalous inputs and outputs.

questions.question: What is the significance of the Executive Order 14110 on Safe, Secure, and Trustworthy AI for AI security practitioners?
options: It bans all AI development in the United States

It establishes requirements for AI safety testing and reporting for the most powerful models, directs NIST to develop AI safety standards, requires red-teaming results be shared with government for dual-use foundation models, and promotes AI security research across federal agencies
It only applies to government AI systems
It requires all AI models to be open source
questions.explanation: Executive Order 14110 (October 2023) has broad implications for AI security: developers of the most powerful models must notify the government and share safety test results, NIST is directed to develop AI safety and security standards, red-teaming is established as an expected practice, and federal agencies are directed to address AI-related security risks. It signals the US government's approach to AI governance and influences industry security practices even beyond the federal sector.

questions.question: What is the 'principle of accountability' in AI governance?
options: That AI systems should be accountable for their own actions without human involvement

That clear human accountability must exist for AI system outcomes -- specific individuals and organizations are responsible for the AI's development, deployment, and impacts, and can be held answerable when things go wrong
That AI usage costs should be accounted for in financial statements
That AI models should keep count of their total interactions
questions.explanation: Accountability in AI governance means that human individuals and organizations bear clear responsibility for AI system outcomes. This includes: who approved the deployment, who monitors the system, who is responsible when the system causes harm, and what remediation is available to affected parties. Without clear accountability, there is no mechanism for governance enforcement, and affected individuals have no recourse when AI systems fail or cause harm.

questions.question: What is OWASP LLM Top 10 category 'Model Denial of Service' (LLM04)?
options: When a model refuses to answer any questions

When attackers craft inputs that consume disproportionate computational resources, degrading service availability for legitimate users through resource exhaustion of the model inference infrastructure
When the model denies the existence of certain facts
When the model provider denies access to paying customers
questions.explanation: OWASP LLM04 (Model Denial of Service) addresses attacks that degrade AI system availability through resource exhaustion. Techniques include: crafting extremely long inputs that maximize processing time, sending inputs that trigger expensive internal operations, flooding the system with concurrent requests, and exploiting inefficient model behaviors. Mitigations include input length limits, request rate limiting, timeout controls, resource quotas, and input complexity analysis.

questions.question: In the context of AI red teaming methodology, what is the difference between 'manual red teaming,' 'automated red teaming,' and 'hybrid approaches'?
options: They are three different names for the same process

Manual red teaming uses human experts to creatively probe for novel failure modes; automated red teaming uses algorithmic methods to systematically test at scale; hybrid approaches combine both, using automation for breadth and coverage while humans provide creativity, context, and judgment for complex failure scenarios
Manual means the model is tested without using a computer
Automated red teaming is always superior to manual testing
questions.explanation: Effective AI red teaming combines approaches: manual red teaming leverages human creativity, domain expertise, and contextual judgment to discover novel, nuanced failure modes that automated tools miss. Automated red teaming provides scalable, systematic coverage across large attack spaces. Hybrid approaches use automation for comprehensive baseline testing and human expertise for creative, context-sensitive probing. The strongest programs integrate both throughout the evaluation lifecycle.

questions.question: What is the purpose of an 'AI acceptable use policy' within an organization?
options: To prevent employees from using any AI tools

To define which AI tools employees may use, how they may use them, what data can and cannot be processed through AI systems, and what approval processes are required for new AI deployments, reducing shadow AI risk and protecting organizational data
To list the AI models that the organization has purchased
To describe the AI team's organizational chart
questions.explanation: An AI acceptable use policy establishes organizational boundaries for AI usage: approved tools and platforms, data classification rules (what can and cannot be submitted to AI systems), required security controls, approval processes for new deployments, and prohibited uses. Without this policy, employees adopt AI tools ad hoc (shadow AI), potentially exposing sensitive data to third-party services or introducing security risks without organizational visibility.

questions.question: Why is it important to maintain version control and rollback capability for deployed AI models?
options: To save storage space by compressing old models

To enable rapid reversion to a known-good model version when a newly deployed model exhibits security vulnerabilities, performance degradation, or harmful behaviors, minimizing the duration of exposure
To track how much the model has improved over time
To comply with font licensing for the model's interface
questions.explanation: Model version control and rollback capability are essential for AI incident response. When a newly deployed model has security issues (backdoor discovered, safety regression, adversarial vulnerability), the ability to immediately roll back to the previous verified-safe version minimizes exposure time. Without rollback capability, the organization must keep the compromised model in production while rushing a fix, extending the window of vulnerability.

questions.question: An organization operates in a regulated industry and must demonstrate compliance with multiple AI governance frameworks (NIST AI RMF, EU AI Act, ISO/IEC 42001). What approach BEST manages multi-framework compliance efficiently?
options: Implementing each framework independently with separate teams and processes

Developing a unified AI governance program that maps common controls across frameworks, implements controls once to satisfy multiple requirements, identifies framework-specific gaps for targeted supplemental controls, and maintains a centralized evidence repository for audits
Ignoring all frameworks until regulators specifically request compliance
Choosing only one framework and hoping the others do not apply
questions.explanation: Multi-framework compliance is most efficiently managed through a unified governance program. Many AI governance frameworks share common requirements (risk assessment, documentation, human oversight, testing). A unified approach maps these overlaps, implements shared controls once, identifies framework-specific requirements for targeted additional work, and maintains centralized evidence that satisfies multiple audit requirements. This avoids duplicated effort while ensuring comprehensive coverage.
